edges.description: Impaired FOXL2-dependent eyelid development produces shortened horizontal palpebral fissures.
Disrupted eyelid morphogenesis produces congenital ptosis in the BPES eyelid complex.
Abnormal eyelid and medial canthal development produces epicanthus inversus.
Abnormal medial canthal development produces telecanthus as part of the congenital BPES eyelid complex.
BPES-associated ocular developmental abnormalities can include strabismus.
Congenital ptosis and associated ocular findings can interfere with visual development and lead to amblyopia.
BPES-associated ocular developmental abnormalities can include refractive errors.
Periocular developmental dysregulation can involve the lacrimal duct system.
FOXL2-related granulosa-cell dysfunction reduces ovarian reserve and predisposes to Type I BPES ovarian failure.
edges.source: FOXL2 dysfunction in ovarian reserve maintenance

edges.description: Type I BPES ovarian dysfunction can produce infertility in affected females.
edges.source: FOXL2 dysfunction in ovarian reserve maintenance
Bone Health Monitoring in Primary Ovarian Insufficiency

edges.target: FOXL2 dysfunction in ovarian reserve maintenance
Polyalanine expansion and FOXL2 protein mislocalization

nodes.description: Amblyopia is a common secondary ophthalmic complication that needs surveillance and treatment during visual development.

evidence.snippet: Other ophthalmic manifestations that can be associated with BPES include dysplastic eyelids, lacrimal duct anomalies, strabismus, refractive errors, and amblyopia.

nodes.description: Ophthalmic follow-up should include visual acuity testing, refraction, and treatment of amblyopia risk through optical correction, occlusion therapy, and strabismus management when indicated.

evidence.snippet: Ophthalmic follow up depends on age, procedures performed in the past, and results of visual acuity testing.

nodes.description: Assisted reproductive technologies are used for fertility planning and treatment in affected women with Type I BPES. Counseling should include fertility preservation options such as oocyte or embryo cryopreservation before ovarian reserve declines, while recognizing that outcomes are heterogeneous across FOXL2 mutation classes.

evidence.snippet: Twenty-one women with BPES were screened for mutations in the FOXL2 gene and underwent assisted reproductive technology (ART) treatment.

nodes.description: Congenital shortening of the horizontal palpebral fissures is one of the four cardinal BPES eyelid features.

evidence.snippet: is defined by a complex eyelid malformation characterized by four major features, all present at birth: blepharophimosis, ptosis, epicanthus inversus, and telecanthus.

nodes.description: Type I females with hypoestrogenism from primary ovarian insufficiency need monitoring and prevention of estrogen-deficiency sequelae, including bone health assessment and coordination with hormone replacement therapy.
nodes.id: Bone Health Monitoring in Primary Ovarian Insufficiency

evidence.reference_title: Bone mineral density in young women with primary ovarian insufficiency: results of a three-year randomized controlled trial of physiological transdermal estradiol and testosterone replacement.
evidence.snippet: Women with primary ovarian insufficiency have significantly lower serum estradiol and T levels compared with regularly menstruating women. They also have significantly reduced bone mineral density (BMD).

nodes.description: Epicanthus inversus is part of the defining congenital eyelid phenotype in both BPES subtypes.

evidence.snippet: is defined by a complex eyelid malformation characterized by four major features, all present at birth: blepharophimosis, ptosis, epicanthus inversus, and telecanthus.
Blepharophimosis, ptosis, and epicanthus inversus syndrome (BPES) is a craniofacial disorder caused by heterozygous variants of the forkhead box L2 (FOXL2) gene.

Blepharophimosis-ptosis-epicanthus inversus syndrome (BPES), caused by FOXL2 variants, has been divided into two subtypes by eyelid abnormalities with (BPES-I) or without (BPES-II) primary ovarian insufficiency (POI).

nodes.description: In affected females with Type I BPES, FOXL2 dysfunction impairs granulosa cell transcriptional regulation and ovarian follicle biology, producing diminished ovarian reserve, adverse assisted reproduction outcomes, and risk for premature ovarian insufficiency.
nodes.id: FOXL2 dysfunction in ovarian reserve maintenance

biological_processes: negative regulation of transcription by RNA polymerase II

evidence.snippet: The mutation abolished the transcriptional repression of FOXL2 on the promoters of CYP19A1 and CCND2 genes, as shown by luciferase reporter assays.
Compared to non-mutation group, patients with FOXL2 mutations had elevated levels of FSH (P=0.007), decreased AMH levels (P=0.012) and less AFC (P=0.015).

nodes.description: Pathogenic FOXL2 variants impair transcriptional control of eyelid developmental targets, including OSR2, and disrupt eyelid morphogenesis. This provides a mechanistic bridge from FOXL2 dysfunction to the congenital blepharophimosis-ptosis-epicanthus inversus phenotype shared by both BPES subtypes.

biological_processes: regulation of transcription by RNA polymerase II

evidence.snippet: Furthermore, the missenses variants c.307C > T; p.(Arg103Cys), c.311A > C; p.(His104Pro), and c.320G > A; p.(Ser107Asn) were not able to transactivate OSR2, which is consistent with the eyelid malformation in these patients.

nodes.description: Female infertility is a clinically important reproductive consequence of Type I BPES.

evidence.snippet: There are two clinical types of BPES: type I patients have eyelid abnormalities accompanied by infertility in affected females, while type II patients only display eyelid malformations.

nodes.description: Surgical ptosis correction, including frontalis suspension approaches, is used to address co-existing blepharoptosis after medial canthal correction. In the traditional staged approach, ptosis correction follows medial canthoplasty by about one year.

evidence.snippet: All cases were subjected to by C plasty with medial and lateral canthoplasty for correction of epicanthus and telecanthus correction followed by frontalis suspension surgery to correct the co-existing blepharoptosis.

nodes.description: Hormone replacement therapy is used to manage Type I BPES-associated premature ovarian insufficiency and hypoestrogenic sequelae.

evidence.snippet: Primary ovarian insufficiency is managed by hormone replacement therapy; fertility is addressed with reproductive technologies such as embryo donation, egg donation, and cryopreservation strategies.

nodes.description: Lacrimal duct anomalies are associated ophthalmic structural findings in BPES and may require ophthalmologic monitoring or intervention.

evidence.snippet: Other ophthalmic manifestations that can be associated with BPES include dysplastic eyelids, lacrimal duct anomalies, strabismus, refractive errors, and amblyopia.

nodes.description: Surgical correction of the medial canthal complex is used to address blepharophimosis, epicanthus inversus, and telecanthus, typically as the first stage of eyelid reconstruction.

evidence.snippet: Eyelid surgery traditionally involves a medial canthoplasty for correction of the blepharophimosis, epicanthus inversus, and telecanthus at age three to five years, typically followed a year later by ptosis correction.

nodes.description: FOXL2 polyalanine-tract expansions and selected missense variants can disrupt normal nuclear localization and promote aggregation or altered intranuclear mobility. This provides a mechanistic route distinct from straightforward truncating-variant haploinsufficiency and is relevant to the BPES-II-enriched polyalanine variant class.
nodes.id: Polyalanine expansion and FOXL2 protein mislocalization

evidence.snippet: Human FOXL2 belongs to the winged helix/forkhead transcription factor family, coding 376 amino acids, which contains a 110 amino acid DNA-binding fork head domain (forkhead domain, FHD) and a polyalanine tract of 14 residues (poly-Ala), whose expansions would cause mislocation, aggregation, and intranuclear mobility of the protein
The most frequent (24.0%, 132/549) variant was p.A225_A234dup.

nodes.description: Ovarian dysfunction in Type I BPES can progress to premature ovarian insufficiency in affected females.

evidence.snippet: BPES type I includes the four major features and primary ovarian insufficiency; BPES type II includes only the four major features.

nodes.description: Bilateral congenital ptosis is part of the cardinal BPES eyelid complex and can interfere with visual development.

evidence.snippet: is defined by a complex eyelid malformation characterized by four major features, all present at birth: blepharophimosis, ptosis, epicanthus inversus, and telecanthus.

nodes.description: Refractive errors are associated ophthalmic findings in BPES and contribute to amblyopia risk when not identified and corrected during visual development.

evidence.snippet: Other ophthalmic manifestations that can be associated with BPES include dysplastic eyelids, lacrimal duct anomalies, strabismus, refractive errors, and amblyopia.

nodes.description: Strabismus is a recurrent associated ophthalmic manifestation in BPES and contributes to amblyopia risk.

evidence.snippet: Other ophthalmic manifestations that can be associated with BPES include dysplastic eyelids, lacrimal duct anomalies, strabismus, refractive errors, and amblyopia.

nodes.description: Telecanthus completes the four-feature congenital eyelid complex that defines BPES.

evidence.snippet: is defined by a complex eyelid malformation characterized by four major features, all present at birth: blepharophimosis, ptosis, epicanthus inversus, and telecanthus.
